AbstractModel robustness against adversarial examples of single perturbation type such as the ℓp-norm has been widely studied.
yet its generalization to more realistic scenarios involving multiple semantic perturbations and their composition remains largely unexplored.

We then propose generalized adversarial training (GAT) to extend model robustness from ℓp-ball to composite semantic perturbations, such as the combination of Hue, Saturation, Brightness, Contrast, and Rotation.

We then propose generalized adversarial training (GAT) to extend model robustness from ℓp-ball to composite semantic perturbations, such as the combination of Hue, Saturation, Brightness, Contrast, and Rotation.
Results obtained using ImageNet and CIFAR-10 datasets indicate that GAT can be robust to any combination of such attacks.

Deep neural networks have shown remarkable success in a wide variety of machine learning (ML) applications.
while these models can achieve outstanding performance on benign data points, recent research has shown that state-of-the-art models can be easily fooled by.
State-of-the-art models can be easily fooled by malicious data points crafted intentionally with adversarial perturbations.
The most effective defense mechanism is to incorporate adversarial examples during model training, known as adversarial training (AT).
Current adversarial training approaches primarily only consider a single perturbation type quantified in a specific distance metric.
The lack of exploration of compositional adversarial robustness against a combination of several threat models could lead to impractical conclusions and undesirable bias in robustness evaluation.

To tackle the issue of lack of exploration of compositional adversarial robustness, the paper proposes generalized adversarial training (GAT), which can harden against a wide range of threat models, from single norms to a combination of them.

Extending standard adversarial training to composite adversarial perturbations is a challenging task, as each perturbation type is sequentially applied, and thus the attack order will affect the effectiveness of the composite adversarial example.

However, natural transformations such as changes in geometry, color, and brightness can also cause adversarial vulnerabilities, leading to what are known as semantic perturbations

Adversarial Training (AT) is a widely adopted method for improving model robustness against adversarial attacks.
Madry et al. proposed to minimize the model.
Madry et al. proposed to minimize the worst-case loss in a region around the input, which is considered as one of the pioneering works in this field.

Zhang et al. further improved AT by considering both natural and adversarial inputs in computing the loss, along with a parameter β to define the ratio of them, leading to a smoother robust decision boundary.
Laidlaw et al. expanded adversarial attacks from single to multiple threat models using neural perceptual distance measurement to generalize adversarial training.
Mao et al. proposed to combine robust components as building blocks of vision transformers, resulting in a state-of-the-art robust vision transformer.
Most previous works on AT have focused on improving model robustness against a single threat model as shown in Fig. 1.
Tramer et al. have considered multiple-norm adversarial training that yields models simultaneously robust against multiple ℓp-norm attacks.

In contrast to prior arts, this paper offers a novel approach to improving model robustness against multiple adversarial threats.
Our attack takes into account the efficient attack order scheduling and extends beyond the ℓp-norm attacks.
Our attack extends beyond the ℓp-norm attacks by incorporating various semantic perturbations.
By incorporating the composite adversarial examples, our defense mechanism can significantly improve the robustness of the model.
Our defense mechanism significantly improves the robustness of the model against adversarial attacks.
Our work represents an important step towards creating more robust deep learning models that can defend against a wide range of adversarial attacks.

The proposed approach can train a model robust to composite adversarial perturbations
GAT is proposed as a generic approach to enhance the robustness of deep learning models against composite semantic perturbations.
GAT aims to prepare classifiers for the real world by enhancing their robustness to composite semantic perturbations.
GAT is designed to achieve state-of-the-art robustness against a wide range of adversarial attacks, including those in ℓp norms and semantic spaces.
GAT was evaluated on CIFAR-10 and ImageNet datasets, demonstrating the highest robust accuracy on most composite attacks.
GAT provides new insights into achieving compositional adversarial robustness, shedding new light on the frontiers of realistic adversarial attacks and defenses.
